metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_1106
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Disruption of the ZnT3 gene, as verified by Northern and Western blots (Fig. 1B-C) as well as by ZnT3 immunochemistry (Fig. 5), leads to reduced total zinc levels in hippocampus and cortex as assessed by plasma emission spectroscopy (Fig. 3). The absence of zinc in synaptic vesicles is confirmed by negative Timm's staining in midbrain sections as well as in mossy fiber boutons (see Fig. 4). Further indirect verification of the absence of ZnT-3 protein on SVs in KO mice ( no detection of ZnT-3 at SVs in nerve terminals by ICC, Fig 5) is provided by quantitative comparison of vesicular zinc levels in hippocampi of WT, +/- and KO animals using TSQ fluorescence (TSQ is a sulfonmide derivative, a membrane permeable Zn2+ indicator that enhances its fluorescence upon binding to Zn2+).
Overall, the authors of the study show that ZnT-3 transporter protein is responsible for Zn2+ loading into synaptic vesicles.. Experimental description: MATERIALS AND METHODS
Generation of ZnT3 Knockout Mice.
